verses.text: And it came to pass, when Solomon had finished the building of the house of the LORD, and the king's house, and all Solomon's desire which he was pleased to do, That the LORD appeared to Solomon the second time, as he had appeared unto him at Gibeon. And the LORD said unto him, I have heard thy prayer and thy supplication, that thou hast made before me: I have hallowed this house, which thou hast built, to put my name there for ever; and mine eyes and mine heart shall be there perpetually. Guidance: The LORD's second theophany to Solomon — the answer to the dedication prayer. The first appearance was at Gibeon (1 Kgs 3:5), where the LORD invited the young king to ask, and Solomon asked for wisdom. This second appearance comes after the temple is finished, the palace is finished, 'all Solomon's desire' (kol cheshek Shelomo — every Solomonic ambition) is finished. The chapter is dating this theophany to the completion of the entire 20-year building program (temple 7 years + palace 13 years; see v10). The LORD's reply is the formal ratification of the dedication prayer of chapter 8. (1) 'I have heard thy prayer and thy supplication.' The LORD has heard. Solomon's seven-scenario prayer was not lost in the cosmic distance; the transcendent God whom heaven cannot contain has fully received the request. (2) 'I have hallowed this house, which thou hast built.' Hebrew hiqdashti — I have made it holy. The cloud-glory of 1 Kgs 8:10-11 was the experiential ratification; this word from the LORD is the verbal ratification. The building is sanctified by divine word. (3) 'To put my name there for ever.' The deuteronomic name-theology made permanent: this is the place. (4) 'Mine eyes and mine heart shall be there perpetually.' The Hebrew einai ve-libbi — my eyes and my heart. The LORD's looking-attention and His loving-attention will rest on this place day after day. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD's commitment to specific places of worship is real and personal, even though He is not contained in them. The temple in Jerusalem received a particular promise of divine attention — eyes and heart — that the Christian believer now finds extended to Christ Himself ('in him dwelleth all the fulness of the Godhead bodily,' Col 2:9) and through Christ to His gathered people. The principle is the same: God's eyes and heart rest on places He has consecrated for His name.

And if thou wilt walk before me, as David thy father walked, in integrity of heart, and in uprightness, to do according to all that I have commanded thee, and wilt keep my statutes and my judgments: Then I will establish the throne of thy kingdom upon Israel for ever, as I promised to David thy father, saying, There shall not fail thee a man upon the throne of Israel. Guidance: The condition. The LORD's promise of dynastic continuance has two halves — promise and condition. The promise: 'I will establish the throne of thy kingdom upon Israel for ever.' The Davidic line will continue. The condition: 'If thou wilt walk before me, as David thy father walked, in integrity of heart, and in uprightness.' The Hebrew be-tom levav u-ve-yosher — in completeness of heart and in straightness. David's life is presented as the standard. David was no morally flawless king — Bathsheba and the census were on his record — but his heart-orientation toward the LORD was consistently faithful. He did not abandon the LORD for other gods. He repented when confronted. His core fidelity remained. Solomon is asked to walk the same way: not flawless, but whole-hearted; not perfect in behavior, but consistent in covenant orientation. 'To do according to all that I have commanded thee, and wilt keep my statutes and my judgments' — keep Torah. The chapter is preserving the basic Old Testament covenant logic. The LORD's promises are sure, but the king's continued participation in them depends on continued obedience. The promise of an eternal Davidic dynasty is not unconditional in its application to specific kings; it is unconditional in its ultimate fulfillment (in Christ) but conditional in the immediate transmission. The chapter is teaching us the difference between God's unilateral covenant of grace (which He always upholds) and His conditional rewards within that covenant (which depend on the obedient response of His servants).

But if ye shall at all turn from following me, ye or your children, and will not keep my commandments and my statutes which I have set before you, but go and serve other gods, and worship them: Then will I cut off Israel out of the land which I have given them; and this house, which I have hallowed for my name, will I cast out of my sight; and Israel shall be a proverb and a byword among all people: And at this house, which is high, every one that passeth by it shall be astonished, and shall hiss; and they shall say, Why hath the LORD done thus unto this land, and to this house? And they shall answer, Because they forsook the LORD their God, who brought forth their fathers out of the land of Egypt, and have taken hold upon other gods, and have worshipped them, and served them: therefore hath the LORD brought upon them all this evil. Guidance: The terrible alternative. The chapter shifts to second-person plural — 'ye or your children.' The LORD is now addressing Israel as a whole through Solomon, not just Solomon as an individual. The condition for catastrophe: 'turn from following me… go and serve other gods.' Apostasy — the worship of other gods — is the single condition that can void the promise. The consequence is fourfold. (1) 'I will cut off Israel out of the land which I have given them.' Exile from the promised land — the most severe national punishment imaginable. (2) 'This house, which I have hallowed for my name, will I cast out of my sight.' The temple itself, just sanctified in v3, would be unsanctified. The LORD would withdraw His eyes and heart from the very building He just promised to attend forever. (3) 'Israel shall be a proverb and a byword among all people.' Hebrew mashal u-sheninah — a proverb and a sharp-saying. Israel would become the world's go-to example of disaster. (4) 'At this house, which is high, every one that passeth by it shall be astonished, and shall hiss.' Hebrew yishrok — hiss, whistle — the ancient gesture of horrified mockery. Travelers approaching the destroyed temple ruins would whistle in shock. Then the imagined Q-and-A: 'Why hath the LORD done thus?' The answer: 'Because they forsook the LORD their God… and have taken hold upon other gods.' The chapter is presenting a sobering vision. The very temple that Solomon has built, the very land Israel inhabits, can become objects of catastrophic divine judgment if covenant fidelity is abandoned. The chapter is recording the warning that Israel did not heed — by 586 BC the temple was rubble, the people exiled to Babylon, and foreign travelers were indeed whistling at the ruins exactly as Solomon was warned. The chapter is teaching us that covenant blessings, however magnificent, do not survive covenant unfaithfulness. The LORD's love is constant; His tolerance of apostasy is not. Both halves of that statement need to be held together. The chapter is honest about both.

And it came to pass at the end of twenty years, when Solomon had built the two houses, the house of the LORD, and the king's house, (Now Hiram the king of Tyre had furnished Solomon with cedar trees and fir trees, and with gold, according to all his desire,) that then king Solomon gave Hiram twenty cities in the land of Galilee. And Hiram came out from Tyre to see the cities which Solomon had given him; and they pleased him not. And he said, What cities are these which thou hast given me, my brother? And he called them the land of Cabul unto this day. And Hiram sent to the king sixscore talents of gold. Guidance: The cities-of-Galilee deal — a small awkward note in the otherwise glorious narrative. After the 20 years of construction, Solomon owes Hiram a substantial settlement beyond the annual wheat and oil shipments. Hiram had supplied not just cedar and fir but also gold (perhaps gold lent for the gilding of the temple interior, perhaps gold as outright supplementary payment). Solomon settles by ceding 20 cities in Galilee — a slice of Israelite territory in the far north handed over to a Phoenician king. The chapter is recording the cession quietly. The Mosaic covenant strictly forbade alienating Israelite land to foreigners (Lev 25:23 — the land belongs to the LORD; the Israelites are tenants, not absolute owners). Solomon's transfer of twenty Galilean cities to Hiram is a small violation. The cities pleased Hiram not. 'What cities are these which thou hast given me, my brother?' His response is diplomatic-disappointed. He calls the area 'the land of Cabul' (eretz Kavul) — the precise meaning is uncertain. Suggestions: 'good for nothing,' 'border-region,' 'displeasing.' The name stuck — it was still called Cabul in the narrator's day. 'Hiram sent to the king sixscore talents of gold' (120 talents — about 4 tons of gold). Whether this was payment in addition to the cities, payment in lieu of accepting the cities as full settlement, or simply a separate transaction, the text is not entirely clear. The chapter is showing us a wrinkle in the Solomon-Hiram alliance. The Phoenician king and the Israelite king did not part with perfect goodwill on the question of compensation. The 20 cities were not first-rate; Hiram was annoyed; the gold was a gesture to settle the matter. The chapter is honest about the awkwardness — even Solomon's diplomacy had imperfect moments.

The chapter is showing us a wrinkle in the Solomon-Hiram alliance. The Phoenician king and the Israelite king did not part with perfect goodwill on the question of compensation. The 20 cities were not first-rate; Hiram was annoyed; the gold was a gesture to settle the matter. The chapter is honest about the awkwardness — even Solomon's diplomacy had imperfect moments.

And this is the reason of the levy which king Solomon raised; for to build the house of the LORD, and his own house, and Millo, and the wall of Jerusalem, and Hazor, and Megiddo, and Gezer. For Pharaoh king of Egypt had gone up, and taken Gezer, and burnt it with fire, and slain the Canaanites that dwelt in the city, and given it for a present unto his daughter, Solomon's wife. And Solomon built Gezer, and Bethhoron the nether, And Baalath, and Tadmor in the wilderness, in the land, And all the cities of store that Solomon had, and cities for his chariots, and cities for his horsemen, and that which Solomon desired to build in Jerusalem, and in Lebanon, and in all the land of his dominion. Guidance: The building program. The narrator now itemizes the projects for which the corvée labor (introduced in 1 Kgs 5:13-18) was used. (1) The temple. (2) Solomon's own palace. (3) Millo — the earthwork-citadel filling the gap between the City of David and the Temple Mount; archaeology has identified the 'stepped stone structure' on the eastern slope of the City of David as part of this construction. (4) The wall of Jerusalem — expanded to incorporate the new temple area. (5) Hazor — the great northern frontier fortress in Naphtali, controlling the route from Damascus. (6) Megiddo — the key chariot-fortress in the Jezreel Valley, controlling the Via Maris. (7) Gezer — the strategic site on the western lowlands. The Gezer parenthesis is striking: Pharaoh of Egypt had attacked Gezer, killed its Canaanite inhabitants, and given the captured site to his daughter as a dowry-present when she married Solomon. The chapter is recording an instance of foreign-policy benefit (Solomon gained Gezer) coupled with the Egyptian-marriage problem (Solomon was deepening the alliance with Egypt through this dowry). Other building projects: Beth-horon the lower (a key pass between the coastal plain and the Jerusalem highlands), Baalath, Tadmor (the famous oasis-city in the Syrian desert, later known as Palmyra; this is one of the most remarkable extents of Solomonic reach), all his store-cities, chariot-cities, and horsemen-cities. The chapter is showing us the militarization of Solomon's kingdom — Megiddo, Hazor, Gezer, Beth-horon, the chariot cities — all are strategic fortresses that turned Israel into a regional power. The chariot-cities in particular violate Deut 17:16 ('he shall not multiply horses'). The chapter is preserving the record without explicit critique but with a clear pattern: Solomon's success is real; Solomon's drift from Mosaic restraint is also real. The same king who built the LORD's house is increasingly building a fortified imperial state that the deuteronomic kingship law had warned against.

And all the people that were left of the Amorites, Hittites, Perizzites, Hivites, and Jebusites, which were not of the children of Israel, Their children that were left after them in the land, whom the children of Israel also were not able utterly to destroy, upon those did Solomon levy a tribute of bondservice unto this day. But of the children of Israel did Solomon make no bondmen: but they were men of war, and his servants, and his princes, and his captains, and rulers of his chariots, and his horsemen. These were the chief of the officers that were over Solomon's work, five hundred and fifty, which bare rule over the people that wrought in the work. Guidance: The two-tier labor system clarified. The chapter draws a sharp legal distinction between two categories of laborers. (1) Non-Israelite descendants of the original Canaanite populations — Amorites, Hittites, Perizzites, Hivites, Jebusites. These groups remained in pockets across the land, never fully subjugated by Joshua's generation. Solomon converts them into permanent forced laborers (mas oved — bondservice tribute). They are slave-status workers, used for the heavy quarrying, hauling, and construction. (2) Israelites. 'Of the children of Israel did Solomon make no bondmen.' Native Israelites were employed in a different category — soldiers, court servants, officers, captains, chariot commanders, horsemen. The Israelite role was supervisory, military, and administrative; the non-Israelite role was menial-physical. (Earlier, 1 Kgs 5:13-14 described 30,000 Israelite levy-workers on rotating one-month tours; the apparent contradiction with v22 may be resolved by distinguishing temporary corvée from permanent slave-status. The Israelite corvée was time-limited service; the Canaanite mas oved was lifelong.) 'These were the chief of the officers that were over Solomon's work, five hundred and fifty, which bare rule over the people that wrought in the work.' (The parallel in 2 Chr 8:10 has 250 Israelite chief officers and additional levels of supervision; the numbers vary by how the supervisory hierarchy is partitioned.) The chapter is recording a stratified labor system that — by modern moral standards — is uncomfortable. The Israelite identity carried preferential treatment; non-Israelite ethnic groups were systematically reduced to permanent servitude. The Bible reports this without explicit critique but the canonical reader hears the discomfort. The prophets would later denounce Israel's tendency to mistreat foreigners (Isa 1:23; Jer 22:13-17); Solomon's labor system was the institutional precedent.

But Pharaoh's daughter came up out of the city of David unto her house which Solomon had built for her: then did he build Millo. And three times in a year did Solomon offer burnt offerings and peace offerings upon the altar which he built unto the LORD, and he burnt incense upon the altar that was before the LORD. So he finished the house. Guidance: Two domestic details. (1) Pharaoh's daughter's relocation. The Egyptian princess had been temporarily housed in the city of David (1 Kgs 3:1) while her own palace was being completed; now she moves into her dedicated residence (the one mentioned in 1 Kgs 7:8). After her move, Solomon proceeds to build the Millo. The chapter is recording the timing — palace first, then the major fortification of Jerusalem. The juxtaposition is suggestive: Pharaoh's daughter has her own permanent residence in the royal compound; Solomon now strengthens the city's fortifications. The chapter is not commenting, but the careful canonical reader notices the deepening Egyptian-Israelite entanglement. (2) Solomon's three annual festivals. 'Three times in a year' refers to the three pilgrimage feasts mandated by Mosaic law (Exod 23:14-17; Deut 16:16): Passover (Pesach), Pentecost (Shavuot), and Tabernacles (Sukkot). At each, Solomon offered burnt offerings and peace offerings on the great bronze altar, and incense on the gold altar inside the temple. The chapter is reporting his liturgical fidelity. Solomon kept the prescribed feast cycle. 'So he finished the house' (ve-shilam et ha-bayit — and he completed the house) — the closing summary. The temple is now complete in every sense: built, dedicated, sanctified by the LORD, and operating with regular sacrificial worship led by the king at the three annual festivals. The chapter is showing us, even as Solomon's foreign policy drifts toward problematic alliances, his liturgical performance remains correct. The danger ahead is not abandonment of the LORD's worship but the addition of other worship alongside it.

And king Solomon made a navy of ships in Eziongeber, which is beside Eloth, on the shore of the Red sea, in the land of Edom. And Hiram sent in the navy his servants, shipmen that had knowledge of the sea, with the servants of Solomon. And they came to Ophir, and fetched from thence gold, four hundred and twenty talents, and brought it to king Solomon. Guidance: The Red Sea fleet — a striking new venture. Solomon builds a navy at Ezion-geber (modern Aqaba on the Gulf of Aqaba, the northeastern arm of the Red Sea, in Edomite territory that Israel controlled). Eloth (also Elath, modern Eilat) is the adjacent town. This was Israel's only access to the Red Sea and beyond — the southern maritime route. Hiram supplied experienced Phoenician sailors to crew the ships alongside Israelite servants. The Israelites were a continental people unfamiliar with deep-sea navigation; the Phoenicians were the master mariners of the eastern Mediterranean. The joint crews sailed to Ophir — the precise location of which is debated by scholars (suggestions include the Arabian peninsula, eastern Africa, or as far as India). Wherever Ophir was, it was famous in the ancient world for high-quality gold. The fleet brought back 420 talents of gold — approximately 16 tons, an enormous quantity. The chapter is showing us Solomon's economic reach. The empire was now generating gold not just from tribute and trade but from active maritime ventures. The Ophir gold would flow into Solomon's treasury for years (1 Kgs 10:11 mentions ongoing shipments). The chapter is recording Solomon's reign at its commercial-zenith. The throne in Jerusalem was now connected by ship to the gold-producing regions of the southern world. Israel, geographically a small inland nation, had through Solomon become a participant in the great trade networks of the ancient world. The chapter is concluding on the note of expanding wealth and reach. The next chapter will follow this thread with the visit of the Queen of Sheba — the moment when the regional fame of Solomon's kingdom would attract its most famous foreign visitor.

Hiram supplied experienced Phoenician sailors to crew the ships alongside Israelite servants. The Israelites were a continental people unfamiliar with deep-sea navigation; the Phoenicians were the master mariners of the eastern Mediterranean. The joint crews sailed to Ophir — the precise location of which is debated by scholars (suggestions include the Arabian peninsula, eastern Africa, or as far as India). Wherever Ophir was, it was famous in the ancient world for high-quality gold. The fleet brought back 420 talents of gold — approximately 16 tons, an enormous quantity. The chapter is showing us Solomon's economic reach. The empire was now generating gold not just from tribute and trade but from active maritime ventures. The Ophir gold would flow into Solomon's treasury for years (1 Kgs 10:11 mentions ongoing shipments). The chapter is recording Solomon's reign at its commercial-zenith. The throne in Jerusalem was now connected by ship to the gold-producing regions of the southern world. Israel, geographically a small inland nation, had through Solomon become a participant in the great trade networks of the ancient world. The chapter is concluding on the note of expanding wealth and reach. The next chapter will follow this thread with the visit of the Queen of Sheba — the moment when the regional fame of Solomon's kingdom would attract its most famous foreign visitor.
